texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Diet and Haemodialysis
texts.text: Diet and Haemodialysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Information for patients
texts.text: Information for patients

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Leeds Teaching Hospitals Trust has a group of renal dietitians who provide a service to all the patients on haemodialysis. We have a commitment to provide you with the most up-to-date dietary information.
texts.text: The Leeds Teaching Hospitals Trust has a group of renal dietitians who provide a service to all the patients on haemodialysis. We have a commitment to provide you with the most up-to-date dietary information.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: What is haemodialysis
texts.text: What is haemodialysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Haemodialysis is the process of removing waste products and excess fluids which build up in the body when the kidneys have stopped working properly.
texts.text: Haemodialysis is the process of removing waste products and excess fluids which build up in the body when the kidneys have stopped working properly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nutrition plays an important role in the treatment of patients on haemodialysis.
texts.text: Nutrition plays an important role in the treatment of patients on haemodialysis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By giving individual advice about your diet we are aiming to:
texts.text: By giving individual advice about your diet we are aiming to:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1.  Improve or maintain your nutritional status and body weight. Having an adequate energy and protein intake plays an important part in keeping you well which helps you to fight infections and respond better to treatments.
texts.text: Improve or maintain your nutritional status and body weight. Having an adequate energy and protein intake plays an important part in keeping you well which helps you to fight infections and respond better to treatments.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.  Keep your blood results within a safe level including:
texts.text: Keep your blood results within a safe level including:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: • Potassium to help protect your heart
texts.text: Potassium to help protect your heart

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Phosphate to help prevent symptoms of itching and longterm complications to your bones, heart and blood vessels
texts.text: Phosphate to help prevent symptoms of itching and longterm complications to your bones, heart and blood vessels

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3.  Reduce salt intake to limit symptoms of thirst and help with blood pressure control
texts.text: Reduce salt intake to limit symptoms of thirst and help with blood pressure control

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4.  Prevent the build-up of fluid between your dialysis sessions
texts.text: Prevent the build-up of fluid between your dialysis sessions

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: What is a 'renal diet'?
texts.text: What is a 'renal diet'?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We are aware that information about the 'renal diet' is available on the internet, from relatives and other patients. There is no single renal diet and the advice offered by the dietitian will be designed to suit your individual needs.
texts.text: We are aware that information about the 'renal diet' is available on the internet, from relatives and other patients. There is no single renal diet and the advice offered by the dietitian will be designed to suit your individual needs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The advice provided will be based on:
texts.text: The advice provided will be based on:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  How well you are eating and what you are eating
texts.text: How well you are eating and what you are eating

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Your monthly blood results
texts.text: Your monthly blood results

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Your body weight
texts.text: Your body weight

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Medications you take
texts.text: Medications you take

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Other dietary needs e.g. diabetes
texts.text: Other dietary needs e.g. diabetes

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •  Whether you still pass urine
texts.text: Whether you still pass urine

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The only foods we suggest all dialysis patients avoid are liver, starfruit, liquorice and cod liver oil tablets.
texts.text: The only foods we suggest all dialysis patients avoid are liver, starfruit, liquorice and cod liver oil tablets.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For further information, please ask the dietitian.
texts.text: For further information, please ask the dietitian.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Other dietary requirements
texts.text: Other dietary requirements

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you have diabetes, coeliac disease, are following a weight reducing diet or have other dietary needs it is important these dietary requirements continue. The dietitian will discuss this with you.
texts.text: If you have diabetes, coeliac disease, are following a weight reducing diet or have other dietary needs it is important these dietary requirements continue. The dietitian will discuss this with you.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Protein
texts.text: Protein

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Protein is an important nutrient needed for the repair of body tissues and to keep your immune system healthy.
texts.text: Protein is an important nutrient needed for the repair of body tissues and to keep your immune system healthy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Protein is lost during every dialysis session. It is therefore important to replace these losses in the diet. If you are unable to eat enough protein to replace these losses and meet your body's needs, you are at risk of reducing the muscle in your body. This increases your risk of malnutrition
texts.text: Protein is lost during every dialysis session. It is therefore important to replace these losses in the diet. If you are unable to eat enough protein to replace these losses and meet your body's needs, you are at risk of reducing the muscle in your body. This increases your risk of malnutrition

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Sources of protein
texts.text: Sources of protein

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Good sources of protein are meat, poultry, fish, eggs, milk, yoghurt, milk pudding, cheese, Quorn, tofu and some soya products.
texts.text: Good sources of protein are meat, poultry, fish, eggs, milk, yoghurt, milk pudding, cheese, Quorn, tofu and some soya products.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: How much protein do I need?
texts.text: How much protein do I need?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The amount of protein your body needs will be discussed with you by your dietitian.
texts.text: The amount of protein your body needs will be discussed with you by your dietitian.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aim to eat protein foods at least twice a day.
texts.text: Aim to eat protein foods at least twice a day.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Personal notes
texts.text: Personal notes

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Energy
texts.text: Energy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Getting enough energy from your food is important. It is needed to make good use of the protein from your diet and for maintaining your target weight. The energy content of food is often called the calorie content. The two main sources of energy (we get) from food are fat and carbohydrates (which includes starches and sugars). A sign that you do not have enough energy (calories) in your diet is weight loss. This may be due to a poor appetite, infection or increased activity. Haemodialysis itself is not thought to increase your energy needs.
texts.text: Getting enough energy from your food is important. It is needed to make good use of the protein from your diet and for maintaining your target weight. The energy content of food is often called the calorie content. The two main sources of energy (we get) from food are fat and carbohydrates (which includes starches and sugars). A sign that you do not have enough energy (calories) in your diet is weight loss. This may be due to a poor appetite, infection or increased activity. Haemodialysis itself is not thought to increase your energy needs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you are losing weight without trying, your dietitian can advise you on ways to increase the energy content of your diet or you may be prescribed some nutritional supplement drinks.
texts.text: If you are losing weight without trying, your dietitian can advise you on ways to increase the energy content of your diet or you may be prescribed some nutritional supplement drinks.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Sources of energy
texts.text: Sources of energy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you have been advised to lose weight then choose the low sugar and reduced fat versions of the foods above.
texts.text: If you have been advised to lose weight then choose the low sugar and reduced fat versions of the foods above.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Understanding 'weight' on dialysis
texts.text: Understanding 'weight' on dialysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A 'target weight' will be set for you within the first few weeks of starting dialysis. Target weight is what your weight is when your body has the right balance of fluid. This is what your weight should be at the end of your dialysis session.
texts.text: A 'target weight' will be set for you within the first few weeks of starting dialysis. Target weight is what your weight is when your body has the right balance of fluid. This is what your weight should be at the end of your dialysis session.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Over time, if you are eating well your flesh (muscle and Fat) weight may increase. Equally, if you are eating poorly your weight may reduce. Should this occur, your target weight will need adjusting. The most accurate way to check this is by using a bedside test called 'BCM' (Body Composition Monitoring).
texts.text: Over time, if you are eating well your flesh (muscle and Fat) weight may increase. Equally, if you are eating poorly your weight may reduce. Should this occur, your target weight will need adjusting. The most accurate way to check this is by using a bedside test called 'BCM' (Body Composition Monitoring).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To help control the amount of fluid weight you gain between each dialysis session, the dietitian will talk to you about salt and fluid.
texts.text: To help control the amount of fluid weight you gain between each dialysis session, the dietitian will talk to you about salt and fluid.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Salt
texts.text: Salt

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A 'reduced salt diet' plays a vital role in the treatment of people requiring haemodialysis. Too much salt in your diet can increase your thirst and make you drink more. This can lead to more fluid building up in your body between dialysis treatments and increase your blood pressure. Ideally, you should not eat more than 6 grams (1 teaspoon) of salt per day. This advice applies to everyone, not just dialysis patients.
texts.text: A 'reduced salt diet' plays a vital role in the treatment of people requiring haemodialysis. Too much salt in your diet can increase your thirst and make you drink more. This can lead to more fluid building up in your body between dialysis treatments and increase your blood pressure. Ideally, you should not eat more than 6 grams (1 teaspoon) of salt per day. This advice applies to everyone, not just dialysis patients.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Simple steps such as not adding salt at the table or in cooking will help, but the most effective way to reduce your salt intake is to eat fewer highly processed foods.
texts.text: Simple steps such as not adding salt at the table or in cooking will help, but the most effective way to reduce your salt intake is to eat fewer highly processed foods.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Where does my salt intake come from?
texts.text: Where does my salt intake come from?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most salt is 'hidden' in food.
texts.text: Most salt is 'hidden' in food.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Salt added to food we buy
texts.text: Salt added to food we buy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Salt added when we cook
texts.text: Salt added when we cook

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Salt added at the table
texts.text: Salt added at the table

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most of the salt we eat is already added to the food we buy. It is found in canned food, jars, ready-made meals and take-away foods. It is important to be able to understand the labels on foods, so that you can make healthier choices.
texts.text: Most of the salt we eat is already added to the food we buy. It is found in canned food, jars, ready-made meals and take-away foods. It is important to be able to understand the labels on foods, so that you can make healthier choices.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: What do the labels mean?
texts.text: What do the labels mean?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The information about salt content on food labels is very useful. However, labels may list the 'salt', or the 'sodium' content, of the food.
texts.text: The information about salt content on food labels is very useful. However, labels may list the 'salt', or the 'sodium' content, of the food.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·  To convert sodium to salt: multiply by 2.5 (e.g. 0.8 g sodium = 2 g salt)
texts.text: To convert sodium to salt: multiply by 2.5 (e.g. 0.8 g sodium = 2 g salt)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·  To convert salt to sodium: divide by 2.5 (e.g. 1 g salt = 0.4 g sodium)
texts.text: To convert salt to sodium: divide by 2.5 (e.g. 1 g salt = 0.4 g sodium)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Front of pack labelling
texts.text: Front of pack labelling

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Traffic Light System
texts.text: Traffic Light System

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This shows at a glance if the food has a high (red), medium (amber/yellow) or low (green) amount of nutrients including salt. Choose foods which are labelled green as much as possible, and keep those labelled red to a minimum.
texts.text: This shows at a glance if the food has a high (red), medium (amber/yellow) or low (green) amount of nutrients including salt. Choose foods which are labelled green as much as possible, and keep those labelled red to a minimum.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on information supplied by the Food Standards Agency (FSA)
texts.text: Based on information supplied by the Food Standards Agency (FSA)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Guideline Daily Amounts (GDA)
texts.text: Guideline Daily Amounts (GDA)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This gives the amount and percentage of nutrients including salt per portion of food of the guideline daily amount (GDA). Use this system for comparing individual foods such as pizzas, breakfast cereals and ready-made meals.
texts.text: This gives the amount and percentage of nutrients including salt per portion of food of the guideline daily amount (GDA). Use this system for comparing individual foods such as pizzas, breakfast cereals and ready-made meals.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Nutritional Information
texts.text: Nutritional Information

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When looking at the labels you need to consider the portion of the food you will be eating. The salt or sodium content of the food will be listed per 100 g of product. Sometimes it may also state the sodium or salt content per serving.
texts.text: When looking at the labels you need to consider the portion of the food you will be eating. The salt or sodium content of the food will be listed per 100 g of product. Sometimes it may also state the sodium or salt content per serving.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: For example
texts.text: For example

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·  For ready meals and sandwiches, aim for a product containing less than 1.25 g salt or 0.5 g sodium per serving.
texts.text: For ready meals and sandwiches, aim for a product containing less than 1.25 g salt or 0.5 g sodium per serving.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·  For individual foods e.g. sauces, snacks and cereals aim for a product containing less than 0.75 g salt or 0.3 g sodium per serving.
texts.text: For individual foods e.g. sauces, snacks and cereals aim for a product containing less than 0.75 g salt or 0.3 g sodium per serving.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Tips on lowering your salt intake
texts.text: Tips on lowering your salt intake

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1.  Cook with fresh foods as often as possible rather than relying on ready-made or processed foods
texts.text: Cook with fresh foods as often as possible rather than relying on ready-made or processed foods

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.  Cut down on the amount of salty ingredients used in cooking e.g. stock cubes, soy sauce, pre-mixed or all purpose seasonings. Instead use low-salt stock cubes or try making your own stock
texts.text: Cut down on the amount of salty ingredients used in cooking e.g. stock cubes, soy sauce, pre-mixed or all purpose seasonings. Instead use low-salt stock cubes or try making your own stock

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3.  Avoid adding salt to your food during cooking and at the table. Instead try black pepper, garlic, herbs, spices, vinegar or lemon juice to add flavour
texts.text: Avoid adding salt to your food during cooking and at the table. Instead try black pepper, garlic, herbs, spices, vinegar or lemon juice to add flavour

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4.  Try to limit your intake of salty meats and fish e.g. bacon, gammon, corned beef and smoked fish to occasional use
texts.text: Try to limit your intake of salty meats and fish e.g. bacon, gammon, corned beef and smoked fish to occasional use

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5.  Avoid salty foods such as crisps, nuts and salted biscuits. Instead choose plain and unsalted varieties
texts.text: Avoid salty foods such as crisps, nuts and salted biscuits. Instead choose plain and unsalted varieties

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6.  Try choosing breakfast cereals which are low in salt or have no added salt such as porridge, Shredded Wheat and Weetabix
texts.text: Try choosing breakfast cereals which are low in salt or have no added salt such as porridge, Shredded Wheat and Weetabix

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 7.  Choose fresh and frozen vegetables where possible. If you use tinned vegetables try buying those tinned in water rather than brine
texts.text: Choose fresh and frozen vegetables where possible. If you use tinned vegetables try buying those tinned in water rather than brine

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 8.  Avoid tinned and packet soups. Instead try homemade soups using onion, garlic, celery and homemade stocks for flavouring. Recipes are available from the dietitian
texts.text: Avoid tinned and packet soups. Instead try homemade soups using onion, garlic, celery and homemade stocks for flavouring. Recipes are available from the dietitian

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 9.  Use lower salt cheese such as cream cheese or cottage cheese and limit your hard cheese intake to 4 oz (120 g) per week
texts.text: Use lower salt cheese such as cream cheese or cottage cheese and limit your hard cheese intake to 4 oz (120 g) per week

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 10.  Salt substitutes are not recommended e.g. LoSalt and supermarket own brands
texts.text: Salt substitutes are not recommended e.g. LoSalt and supermarket own brands

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Homemade gravy:   Add a cup full of water or wine to the cooking juices from your meat. Add black pepper (plus some herbs if liked) then thicken with cornflour if required.
texts.text: Homemade gravy:   Add a cup full of water or wine to the cooking juices from your meat. Add black pepper (plus some herbs if liked) then thicken with cornflour if required.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Fluid
texts.text: Fluid

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When you first start dialysis, you will probably still be passing a normal amount of urine. Over the next few months (or longer), you may notice that you pass much less urine. This isn't the same for everyone and it is therefore important to let the nursing staff on the dialysis unit know if your urine output changes.
texts.text: When you first start dialysis, you will probably still be passing a normal amount of urine. Over the next few months (or longer), you may notice that you pass much less urine. This isn't the same for everyone and it is therefore important to let the nursing staff on the dialysis unit know if your urine output changes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you drink more than you are passing or can be removed by dialysis, the excess fluid will build up in your body causing fluid overload. This excess fluid will make your heart work harder. It may also cause an increase in blood pressure, swelling around the ankles and shortness of breath. To prevent excess fluid building up you will be advised on how much fluid you can drink in a day; this is called a 'fluid allowance'.
texts.text: If you drink more than you are passing or can be removed by dialysis, the excess fluid will build up in your body causing fluid overload. This excess fluid will make your heart work harder. It may also cause an increase in blood pressure, swelling around the ankles and shortness of breath. To prevent excess fluid building up you will be advised on how much fluid you can drink in a day; this is called a 'fluid allowance'.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to work out your fluid allowance the nursing staff will ask you to complete a 24 hour urine collection. Your fluid allowance will be given in either litres (L) or millilitres (mls).
texts.text: In order to work out your fluid allowance the nursing staff will ask you to complete a 24 hour urine collection. Your fluid allowance will be given in either litres (L) or millilitres (mls).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dietitian will be able to advise you on ways to reduce the amount of fluid you take from drinks and foods, and give practical tips on managing your allowance.
texts.text: The dietitian will be able to advise you on ways to reduce the amount of fluid you take from drinks and foods, and give practical tips on managing your allowance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Following a low salt diet will help you to control your fluid intake as a diet high in salt will make you thirsty.
texts.text: Following a low salt diet will help you to control your fluid intake as a diet high in salt will make you thirsty.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Potassium
texts.text: Potassium

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Not every patient on haemodialysis needs to follow a low potassium diet. This is because potassium is easily removed on dialysis. If your potassium level goes too high or too low it can cause an abnormal heart beat. This increases the risk of cardiac arrest.
texts.text: Not every patient on haemodialysis needs to follow a low potassium diet. This is because potassium is easily removed on dialysis. If your potassium level goes too high or too low it can cause an abnormal heart beat. This increases the risk of cardiac arrest.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you have previously been advised to follow a low potassium diet, continue to do so until told otherwise.
texts.text: If you have previously been advised to follow a low potassium diet, continue to do so until told otherwise.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If your potassium level goes too high or too low, the nursing staff or doctors will refer you to the dietitian for individualised dietary advice to assess your potassium intake.
texts.text: If your potassium level goes too high or too low, the nursing staff or doctors will refer you to the dietitian for individualised dietary advice to assess your potassium intake.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The recommended range for your blood potassium before
texts.text: The recommended range for your blood potassium before

texts.label: text
texts.orig: dialysis is:
texts.text: dialysis is:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Phosphate
texts.text: Phosphate

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When you have too much phosphate in your blood it can cause the body to pull calcium from your bones. This makes bones weaker and the calcium can get deposited in your blood vessels, leading to cardiovascular disease.
texts.text: When you have too much phosphate in your blood it can cause the body to pull calcium from your bones. This makes bones weaker and the calcium can get deposited in your blood vessels, leading to cardiovascular disease.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are 3 ways of controlling your phosphate, these are known as the 3 D's:
texts.text: There are 3 ways of controlling your phosphate, these are known as the 3 D's:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1.  Dialysis
texts.text: Dialysis

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.  Diet
texts.text: Diet

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3.  Drugs (phosphate binders)
texts.text: Drugs (phosphate binders)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If your phosphate is high the nursing staff or doctor will refer you to the dietitian for advice on a low phosphate diet. Tablets referred to as phosphate binders may also be
texts.text: If your phosphate is high the nursing staff or doctor will refer you to the dietitian for advice on a low phosphate diet. Tablets referred to as phosphate binders may also be

texts.label: text
texts.orig: prescribed to help control your phosphate level.
texts.text: prescribed to help control your phosphate level.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The recommended range for your blood phosphate before
texts.text: The recommended range for your blood phosphate before

texts.label: text
texts.orig: dialysis is:
texts.text: dialysis is:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Vitamins and minerals
texts.text: Vitamins and minerals

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All patients on haemodialysis are prescribed a vitamin supplement. These are given to help replace the vitamins lost during dialysis. You may be given Renavit or a combination of Folic Acid and Vitamin B compound strong .
texts.text: All patients on haemodialysis are prescribed a vitamin supplement. These are given to help replace the vitamins lost during dialysis. You may be given Renavit or a combination of Folic Acid and Vitamin B compound strong .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We do not recommend the purchase of over the counter multivitamins. If you take any vitamin or mineral supplement, herbal remedies or anything else that your doctor does not prescribe, it is important you let your doctor, pharmacist or dietitian know.
texts.text: We do not recommend the purchase of over the counter multivitamins. If you take any vitamin or mineral supplement, herbal remedies or anything else that your doctor does not prescribe, it is important you let your doctor, pharmacist or dietitian know.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If you have any suggestions, comments or queries regarding this leaflet please let your dietitian know.
texts.text: If you have any suggestions, comments or queries regarding this leaflet please let your dietitian know.
